Limits and Continuity.skill: CHA-1.A Limits and Continuity - 1.1 Introducing Calculus: Can Change Occur at an Instant?
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic introduces AP Calculus AB students to the foundational question of calculus: can change occur at an instant? (CED: CHA-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students explore how average rates of change over progressively smaller intervals approach an instantaneous rate of change. Key concepts include understanding that calculus provides tools to analyze instantaneous change, distinguishing between average and instantaneous rates, and recognizing that the limit process bridges the gap between these two ideas. Students should connect this to real-world contexts such as velocity (instantaneous speed vs. average speed), population growth rates, and temperature change. The difference quotient [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) represents average rate of change; as the interval shrinks, it approaches instantaneous rate. This topic sets the stage for limits and derivatives. AP exam note: questions may ask students to interpret rates of change in context and explain what an instantaneous rate represents. Students should be comfortable with graphical, numerical, and verbal representations of change. Vocabulary: average rate of change, instantaneous rate of change, secant line, difference quotient, interval.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.A, LIM-1.B Limits and Continuity - 1.2 Defining Limits and Using Limit Notation
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers defining limits and using proper limit notation for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.A, LIM-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students learn that the limit of f(x) as x approaches c, written lim[x->c] f(x) = L, means f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L by choosing x sufficiently close to c (but not equal to c). Key concepts: the limit describes the behavior of a function near a point, not necessarily at the point; one-sided limits (left-hand limit and right-hand limit); a two-sided limit exists if and only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal. Students should understand limit notation: lim[x->c-] f(x) for left-hand and lim[x->c+] f(x) for right-hand limits. The limit may exist even when f(c) is undefined or differs from L. Students should interpret limits graphically, numerically, and analytically. AP exam note: multiple-choice questions frequently test whether students can determine limits from graphs or tables and use correct notation. Vocabulary: limit, left-hand limit, right-hand limit, two-sided limit, limit notation, approaches.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.C Limits and Continuity - 1.3 Estimating Limit Values from Graphs
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers estimating limit values from graphs for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students learn to read graphs to determine limits as x approaches a value from the left and right. Key concepts: identifying the y-value a function approaches (not necessarily reaches) as x gets close to a given value; recognizing when left-hand and right-hand limits differ (limit does not exist); identifying cases where the function value at a point differs from the limit; and recognizing unbounded behavior (limits involving infinity). Students must distinguish between f(c) and lim[x->c] f(x), noting that open circles on graphs indicate the function is not defined or takes a different value at that point. Common pitfalls include confusing the function value with the limit and misreading scale. AP exam note: the AP exam regularly presents piecewise or discontinuous graphs and asks students to evaluate limits. Students should practice with graphs that have jumps, holes, and vertical asymptotes. Vocabulary: graphical limit, open circle, closed circle, piecewise function, jump discontinuity.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.C Limits and Continuity - 1.4 Estimating Limit Values from Tables
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers estimating limit values from tables of data for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students use numerical data to estimate limits by examining function values at inputs increasingly close to the target value from both sides. Key concepts: constructing or reading tables with x-values approaching c from the left (c-0.1, c-0.01, ...) and right (c+0.1, c+0.01, ...); observing the trend in f(x) values to estimate the limit; recognizing when the limit does not exist from numerical evidence (e.g., oscillation or divergent values). Students should understand that tables provide estimates, not proofs, of limits. Common errors include choosing x-values too far from c or failing to check both sides. This connects to Topic 1.3 (graphical estimation) and prepares students for algebraic limit evaluation. AP exam note: free-response questions may provide tabular data and ask students to estimate limits and explain their reasoning. Vocabulary: numerical estimate, table of values, approaching, left-hand values, right-hand values, trend.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.D Limits and Continuity - 1.5 Determining Limits Using Algebraic Properties of Limits
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers the algebraic properties of limits for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students learn that if lim[x->c] f(x) and lim[x->c] g(x) both exist, then limits preserve arithmetic operations: the limit of a sum is the sum of the limits, the limit of a product is the product of the limits, the limit of a quotient is the quotient of the limits (provided the denominator limit is nonzero), and the limit of a constant times a function equals the constant times the limit. Additional properties: lim[x->c] [f(x)]^n = [lim[x->c] f(x)]^n and the limit of a composite function under appropriate conditions. Students should also know that the limit of a constant is the constant itself, and lim[x->c] x = c (direct substitution for polynomials and rational functions where the denominator is nonzero). These properties allow students to evaluate limits of complex expressions by breaking them into simpler parts. AP exam note: mastery of these properties is essential for efficient limit computation. Vocabulary: limit laws, sum rule, product rule, quotient rule, power rule, direct substitution.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.E Limits and Continuity - 1.6 Determining Limits Using Algebraic Manipulation
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers determining limits using algebraic manipulation for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.E, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). When direct substitution yields an indeterminate form like 0/0, students must manipulate the expression algebraically before evaluating the limit. Key techniques: factoring and canceling common factors, multiplying by the conjugate (for expressions involving radicals), simplifying complex fractions, expanding and combining like terms, and using trigonometric identities. For example, lim[x->3] (x^2-9)/(x-3) = lim[x->3] (x+3) = 6 after factoring. Students should also know the foundational limits: lim[x->0] sin(x)/x = 1 and lim[x->0] (1-cos(x))/x = 0. The goal is to transform the expression into one where direct substitution works. AP exam note: algebraic limit manipulation is heavily tested in both multiple-choice and free-response sections. Students should recognize which technique to apply based on the form of the expression. Vocabulary: indeterminate form, factoring, conjugate, rationalize, simplify, cancel.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.D, LIM-1.E Limits and Continuity - 1.7 Selecting Procedures for Determining Limits
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers selecting appropriate procedures for determining limits in AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.D, LIM-1.E, Unit 1, Topic 1.7). Students must strategically choose among multiple methods: direct substitution (try first), factoring, conjugate multiplication, trigonometric identities, or simplifying complex fractions. The decision process: (1) attempt direct substitution; (2) if the result is a finite number, that is the limit; (3) if the result is nonzero/zero, the limit is infinite or does not exist; (4) if the result is 0/0 (indeterminate), apply algebraic manipulation. Students should also consider graphical or numerical methods when algebraic approaches are difficult. This topic synthesizes skills from Topics 1.2-1.6 and emphasizes mathematical reasoning about which approach is most efficient. AP exam note: the exam tests procedural fluency and the ability to choose the right technique quickly. Students should practice switching between methods when one approach stalls. Vocabulary: direct substitution, indeterminate form, procedure selection, factoring, conjugate, algebraic manipulation.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.E Limits and Continuity - 1.8 Determining Limits Using the Squeeze Theorem
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers the Squeeze Theorem (also called the Sandwich Theorem) for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.E, Unit 1, Topic 1.8). The theorem states: if g(x) <= f(x) <= h(x) for all x near c (except possibly at c), and lim[x->c] g(x) = lim[x->c] h(x) = L, then lim[x->c] f(x) = L. This is especially useful for limits involving oscillating functions, such as lim[x->0] x^2 sin(1/x) = 0, where -x^2 <= x^2 sin(1/x) <= x^2 and both bounds approach 0. Key concepts: identifying appropriate bounding functions, verifying the inequality holds near the point, confirming the bounding functions share the same limit. Students should understand when the Squeeze Theorem is necessary (typically when algebraic manipulation fails, often involving trigonometric or oscillating expressions). AP exam note: the Squeeze Theorem appears occasionally on the AP exam, usually in multiple-choice questions. Students should be prepared to both apply the theorem and justify its use. Vocabulary: Squeeze Theorem, Sandwich Theorem, bounding functions, inequality, oscillating function.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-1.A, LIM-1.B, LIM-1.C Limits and Continuity - 1.9 Connecting Multiple Representations of Limits
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers connecting graphical, numerical, and analytical representations of limits for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-1.A, LIM-1.B, LIM-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.9). Students should fluidly move between representations: reading limits from graphs (visual behavior near a point), estimating from tables (numerical trends), and computing algebraically (analytical techniques). Key concepts: a limit found graphically should be confirmed analytically when possible; numerical estimates provide evidence but not proof; analytical methods give exact values. Students should reconcile information when representations appear to conflict, understanding that each representation has limitations (e.g., graphs may have misleading scale, tables may miss oscillation). This topic emphasizes the AP Calculus principle that mathematical understanding requires connecting multiple representations. AP exam note: free-response questions often present information in one representation and ask students to interpret or verify in another. Students should practice translating between graphs, tables, equations, and verbal descriptions of limits. Vocabulary: multiple representations, graphical, numerical, analytical, verbal, consistency, verification.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-2.A Limits and Continuity - 1.10 Exploring Types of Discontinuities
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers types of discontinuities for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.10). Students learn to classify discontinuities: (1) Removable (hole): lim[x->c] f(x) exists but does not equal f(c) or f(c) is undefined; the discontinuity can be 'removed' by redefining f(c). (2) Jump: the left-hand and right-hand limits both exist but are not equal. (3) Infinite: the function increases or decreases without bound as x approaches c, producing a vertical asymptote. Key concepts: identifying each type from graphs and algebraic expressions, understanding that removable discontinuities correspond to common factors in rational functions, and recognizing that piecewise functions may have jump discontinuities at boundary points. Students should connect this to continuity (Topic 1.11): a function is continuous at c if none of these discontinuities occur. AP exam note: students may be asked to identify and classify discontinuities from graphs or formulas. Vocabulary: removable discontinuity, jump discontinuity, infinite discontinuity, hole, vertical asymptote, piecewise function.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-2.A Limits and Continuity - 1.11 Defining Continuity at a Point
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers the formal definition of continuity at a point for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.11). A function f is continuous at x = c if and only if three conditions hold: (1) f(c) is defined, (2) lim[x->c] f(x) exists, and (3) lim[x->c] f(x) = f(c). If any condition fails, f is discontinuous at c. Key concepts: applying the three-part definition to determine continuity, understanding that polynomials, exponentials, and trigonometric functions are continuous on their domains, and recognizing that rational functions are continuous where the denominator is nonzero. Students should be able to determine values of parameters that make piecewise functions continuous by setting left-hand and right-hand limits equal at boundary points. AP exam note: the AP exam frequently asks students to find parameter values ensuring continuity of piecewise functions and to justify continuity using the three-part definition. Vocabulary: continuous at a point, defined, limit exists, limit equals function value, piecewise continuity, parameter.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-2.B Limits and Continuity - 1.12 Confirming Continuity over an Interval
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers confirming continuity over an interval for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-2.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.12). A function is continuous on an open interval (a,b) if it is continuous at every point in the interval. For a closed interval [a,b], continuity additionally requires right-continuity at a (lim[x->a+] f(x) = f(a)) and left-continuity at b (lim[x->b-] f(x) = f(b)). Key concepts: polynomials are continuous everywhere; rational, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions are continuous on their domains; compositions and combinations of continuous functions are continuous where defined. Students should identify intervals of continuity from graphs and formulas and verify continuity conditions at endpoints. This provides the foundation for applying the Intermediate Value Theorem (Topic 1.16) and later the Extreme Value Theorem. AP exam note: students should be able to state the interval(s) on which a function is continuous and justify their answer. Vocabulary: continuous on an interval, open interval, closed interval, right-continuous, left-continuous, domain.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-2.C Limits and Continuity - 1.13 Removing Discontinuities
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers removing discontinuities for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-2.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.13). A removable discontinuity at x = c can be 'removed' by defining or redefining f(c) = lim[x->c] f(x). For rational functions, removable discontinuities occur where a common factor in the numerator and denominator cancels, leaving a hole in the graph. Key concepts: identifying removable vs. non-removable discontinuities, factoring to find and cancel common factors, determining the y-coordinate of the hole by evaluating the simplified function at c, and extending a function to make it continuous. Students should recognize that only removable discontinuities can be fixed by redefining a single function value; jump and infinite discontinuities cannot be removed this way. AP exam note: questions may ask students to find the value that makes a piecewise function continuous at a boundary or to identify coordinates of holes in rational functions. Vocabulary: removable discontinuity, hole, redefine, extend continuously, common factor, continuous extension.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-2.D Limits and Continuity - 1.14 Connecting Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers infinite limits and vertical asymptotes for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-2.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.14). When lim[x->c] f(x) = +infinity or -infinity, f has a vertical asymptote at x = c. Key concepts: the limit does not exist in the finite sense when the function grows without bound; one-sided infinite limits (function may go to +infinity from one side and -infinity from the other); for rational functions p(x)/q(x), vertical asymptotes occur at values where q(c) = 0 but p(c) != 0; sign analysis near the asymptote determines whether the function goes to +infinity or -infinity. Students should analyze the behavior of f(x) near vertical asymptotes by examining the sign of the numerator and denominator separately. Logarithmic functions also have vertical asymptotes (e.g., ln(x) as x->0+). AP exam note: students should clearly communicate that infinite limits mean the limit does not exist (as a finite number) while describing the unbounded behavior. Vocabulary: infinite limit, vertical asymptote, unbounded, sign analysis, one-sided limit, grows without bound.

Limits and Continuity.skill: LIM-2.D Limits and Continuity - 1.15 Connecting Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-2.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.15). If lim[x->infinity] f(x) = L or lim[x->-infinity] f(x) = L, then y = L is a horizontal asymptote. For rational functions p(x)/q(x): if degree of p < degree of q, the horizontal asymptote is y = 0; if degrees are equal, the asymptote is y = (leading coefficient of p)/(leading coefficient of q); if degree of p > degree of q, there is no horizontal asymptote (the limit is infinite). Key techniques: divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x in the denominator. Students should also understand end behavior of exponential functions (e^x -> infinity, e^(-x) -> 0), logarithmic functions, and compositions. A function can cross its horizontal asymptote; the asymptote describes end behavior only. AP exam note: comparing growth rates of functions (exponential dominates polynomial dominates logarithmic) is important. Vocabulary: limit at infinity, horizontal asymptote, end behavior, dominant term, growth rate, leading coefficient.

Limits and Continuity.skill: FUN-1.A Limits and Continuity - 1.16 Working with the Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
Limits and Continuity.prompt: This topic covers the Intermediate Value Theorem for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.16). The IVT states: if f is continuous on [a,b] and N is any value between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a,b) such that f(c) = N. Key concepts: the IVT guarantees existence of a value but does not locate it precisely; continuity on the closed interval is a required hypothesis; the theorem is often used to show that a function has a zero (root) on an interval by showing f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs. Students should be able to state the theorem precisely, verify its hypotheses, and apply it to justify the existence of solutions. The IVT connects to the idea that continuous functions cannot 'skip' values. AP exam note: free-response questions frequently ask students to invoke the IVT, requiring explicit mention of continuity and that the target value lies between f(a) and f(b). Vocabulary: Intermediate Value Theorem, continuous, existence, root, zero, closed interval.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: CHA-2.A, CHA-2.B Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.1 Defining Average and Instantaneous Rates of Change at a Point
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers defining average and instantaneous rates of change for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-2.A, CHA-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). The average rate of change of f on [a,b] is [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a), which is the slope of the secant line through (a,f(a)) and (b,f(b)). The instantaneous rate of change at x = a is the limit of the average rate of change as the interval shrinks: lim[h->0] [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h. Key concepts: secant lines approximate tangent lines as endpoints get closer; the instantaneous rate of change is the slope of the tangent line at a point; interpreting rates of change in real-world contexts (velocity, growth rate, marginal cost). Students should compute average rates from functions, tables, and graphs, and understand that the instantaneous rate requires a limiting process. AP exam note: students must be able to interpret the meaning of average and instantaneous rates in context, including units. Vocabulary: average rate of change, instantaneous rate of change, secant line, tangent line, difference quotient, slope.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: CHA-2.B, CHA-2.C Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.2 Defining the Derivative of a Function and Using Derivative Notation
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers the definition of the derivative using limits for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-2.B, CHA-2.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). The derivative of f at x = a is defined as f'(a) = lim[h->0] [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h, equivalently lim[x->a] [f(x)-f(a)]/(x-a). When this limit exists, it gives the instantaneous rate of change and the slope of the tangent line at x = a. The derivative function f'(x) is defined by applying this limit for general x. Key notation: f'(x), dy/dx, d/dx[f(x)], and y'. Students should compute derivatives from the limit definition for polynomial and simple functions. The derivative at a point can also be interpreted as the best linear approximation of f near that point. AP exam note: free-response questions may require students to set up and evaluate the limit definition, or explain what a derivative represents in context. Vocabulary: derivative, limit definition, difference quotient, instantaneous rate of change, tangent line, Leibniz notation, prime notation.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: CHA-2.D Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.3 Estimating Derivatives of a Function at a Point
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers estimating derivatives at a point for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-2.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students estimate f'(a) using slopes of secant lines from tables or graphs. From a table, use the difference quotient with values near a: f'(a) is approximately [f(a+h)-f(a)]/h for small h, or use the symmetric difference quotient [f(a+h)-f(a-h)]/(2h) for a better estimate. From a graph, estimate the slope of the tangent line by drawing it and computing rise/run. Key concepts: the estimate improves as the interval shrinks; the symmetric difference quotient often gives a more accurate estimate; students should choose appropriate nearby points from the table. This skill connects numerical and graphical understanding to the analytical definition of the derivative. AP exam note: free-response questions frequently provide tables of values and ask students to approximate a derivative, often requiring units and contextual interpretation. Vocabulary: estimate, secant line, difference quotient, symmetric difference quotient, slope, approximation.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-2.A Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.4 Connecting Differentiability and Continuity: Determining When Derivatives Do and Do Not Exist
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between differentiability and continuity for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Key theorem: if f is differentiable at x = c, then f is continuous at c. The converse is false: continuity does not guarantee differentiability. A function is not differentiable at points where: (1) there is a corner or cusp (left and right derivatives differ), (2) there is a vertical tangent (slope is infinite), (3) there is a discontinuity. Students should identify non-differentiable points from graphs by looking for sharp turns, vertical tangent lines, and jumps. Differentiability on an interval means the derivative exists at every point in the interval. Piecewise functions require checking that left-hand and right-hand derivatives match at boundary points. AP exam note: students may be asked to determine where a function is differentiable and justify using the relationship between differentiability and continuity. Vocabulary: differentiable, continuous, corner, cusp, vertical tangent, piecewise, left-hand derivative, right-hand derivative.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-3.A Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.5 Applying the Power Rule
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers the Power Rule for differentiation in AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). The Power Rule states: if f(x) = x^n, then f'(x) = n*x^(n-1) for any real number n. This extends to functions like f(x) = x^(1/2) (square root), f(x) = x^(-1) (reciprocal), and other rational and negative exponents. Students should rewrite radicals and reciprocals as power functions before applying the rule. Key applications: d/dx[x^3] = 3x^2, d/dx[sqrt(x)] = d/dx[x^(1/2)] = (1/2)x^(-1/2), d/dx[1/x^2] = d/dx[x^(-2)] = -2x^(-3). Combined with constant multiple and sum/difference rules (Topic 2.6), the Power Rule enables differentiation of all polynomial functions. Students should practice both finding derivatives and rewriting functions into power-rule-ready form. AP exam note: the Power Rule is foundational and is used extensively throughout the course. Errors in applying it (especially with negative and fractional exponents) are common. Vocabulary: Power Rule, exponent, polynomial, rational exponent, negative exponent, rewrite.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-3.A Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.6 Derivative Rules: Constant, Sum, Difference, and Constant Multiple
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers basic derivative rules for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Rules: (1) Constant Rule: d/dx[c] = 0 for any constant c. (2) Constant Multiple Rule: d/dx[c*f(x)] = c*f'(x). (3) Sum Rule: d/dx[f(x)+g(x)] = f'(x)+g'(x). (4) Difference Rule: d/dx[f(x)-g(x)] = f'(x)-g'(x). These rules, combined with the Power Rule, allow differentiation of any polynomial: d/dx[3x^4 - 5x^2 + 7x - 2] = 12x^3 - 10x + 7. Students should understand that differentiation is a linear operation (it preserves addition and scalar multiplication). Key applications include finding equations of tangent lines, determining rates of change, and setting derivatives equal to zero to find critical points. AP exam note: these fundamental rules are used in virtually every derivative computation on the exam. Students must apply them fluently and accurately. Vocabulary: constant rule, constant multiple rule, sum rule, difference rule, linear operator, polynomial derivative.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-3.A, LIM-3.A Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.7 Derivatives of cos x, sin x, eˣ, and ln x
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers derivatives of basic transcendental functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.A, LIM-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Key formulas: d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = -sin x, d/dx[e^x] = e^x, d/dx[ln x] = 1/x. Students should memorize these derivatives and understand their derivations: the sine derivative uses lim[h->0] sin(h)/h = 1 and lim[h->0] (cos(h)-1)/h = 0; the exponential derivative follows from the definition of e as the base for which d/dx[e^x] = e^x; the natural log derivative follows from implicit differentiation of y = ln x (e^y = x). These transcendental derivatives appear frequently in combination with the product, quotient, and chain rules in later topics. AP exam note: these four derivatives are among the most commonly tested on the AP exam. Students should be able to combine them with other rules fluently. Vocabulary: transcendental function, sine, cosine, exponential, natural logarithm, derivative formula.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-3.B Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.8 The Product Rule
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers the Product Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.8). If f and g are differentiable, then d/dx[f(x)*g(x)] = f'(x)*g(x) + f(x)*g'(x). In Leibniz notation: d/dx[uv] = u'v + uv'. Key concepts: the derivative of a product is NOT the product of the derivatives; students must apply the rule correctly by differentiating each factor separately and summing. Students should practice with products involving polynomials, trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions. Example: d/dx[x^2 sin x] = 2x sin x + x^2 cos x. The Product Rule can be extended to three or more factors. It is also useful in deriving other rules (e.g., the Quotient Rule can be derived from the Product Rule). AP exam note: the Product Rule is frequently tested both in isolation and in combination with the Chain Rule. Students should simplify their answers when possible. Vocabulary: Product Rule, factors, differentiate, derivative of a product, Leibniz notation.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-3.B Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.9 The Quotient Rule
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers the Quotient Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.9). If f and g are differentiable and g(x) != 0, then d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)*g(x) - f(x)*g'(x)] / [g(x)]^2. Memory aid: 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared.' Key concepts: the derivative of a quotient is NOT the quotient of the derivatives; the order of subtraction matters (it is not commutative); the denominator is always the square of the original denominator. Students should practice with rational functions and quotients involving transcendental functions. Example: d/dx[sin x / x] = [cos x * x - sin x * 1]/x^2. Sometimes rewriting a quotient using negative exponents and the Product Rule is an alternative approach. AP exam note: the Quotient Rule is commonly tested; sign errors and order errors are frequent mistakes. Students should verify their answers by checking special cases. Vocabulary: Quotient Rule, numerator, denominator, rational function, lo-d-hi, simplify.

Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.skill: FUN-3.B Differentiation: Definition and Basic Derivative Rules - 2.10 Finding the Derivatives of Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, and/or Cosecant Functions
Differentiation: Definition and Basic De.prompt: This topic covers derivatives of the remaining trigonometric functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.10). Key formulas: d/dx[tan x] = sec^2 x, d/dx[cot x] = -csc^2 x, d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x, d/dx[csc x] = -csc x cot x. These are derived using the Quotient Rule applied to sin x/cos x (for tangent), cos x/sin x (for cotangent), 1/cos x (for secant), and 1/sin x (for cosecant). Students should memorize these formulas and notice the pattern: co-function derivatives have negative signs. These derivatives frequently appear in combination with the Chain Rule (e.g., d/dx[tan(3x)] = 3 sec^2(3x)). Understanding the domains of these functions (avoiding zeros of sin x and cos x) is important. AP exam note: while these derivatives appear less frequently than sin and cos, they are commonly tested in multiple-choice and are essential for integration in later units. Vocabulary: tangent, cotangent, secant, cosecant, Quotient Rule derivation, co-function, domain restriction.

Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.skill: FUN-3.C Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions - 3.1 The Chain Rule
Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.prompt: This topic covers the Chain Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). The Chain Rule states: if y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). In words: differentiate the outer function (evaluated at the inner function) and multiply by the derivative of the inner function. In Leibniz notation: if y = f(u) and u = g(x), then dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx). Key concepts: identifying the outer and inner functions in a composition, applying the Chain Rule with any combination of functions (polynomial, trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic), and using the Chain Rule in combination with Product and Quotient Rules. Examples: d/dx[sin(x^2)] = cos(x^2)*2x; d/dx[e^(3x)] = 3e^(3x); d/dx[ln(x^2+1)] = 2x/(x^2+1). The Chain Rule extends to multiple compositions. AP exam note: the Chain Rule is one of the most important and frequently tested rules on the AP exam. Vocabulary: Chain Rule, composition, outer function, inner function, nested functions.

Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.skill: FUN-3.D Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions - 3.2 Implicit Differentiation
Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.prompt: This topic covers implicit differentiation for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). When a relation is given implicitly (e.g., x^2 + y^2 = 25), rather than solved for y explicitly, students differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as a function of x and applying the Chain Rule: d/dx[y^2] = 2y(dy/dx). Then solve for dy/dx algebraically. Key concepts: every term involving y requires the Chain Rule (multiply by dy/dx); the resulting expression for dy/dx typically involves both x and y; use the original equation to simplify when possible. Applications include finding slopes of tangent lines to curves like circles, ellipses, and other implicit relations. Example: for x^2 + y^2 = 25, differentiating gives 2x + 2y(dy/dx) = 0, so dy/dx = -x/y. AP exam note: implicit differentiation appears regularly on the exam, including in related rates and analyzing implicit relations. Students must show clear work with dy/dx terms. Vocabulary: implicit differentiation, implicit relation, explicit function, Chain Rule, tangent line to a curve.

Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.skill: FUN-3.D Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions - 3.3 Differentiating Inverse Functions
Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.prompt: This topic covers differentiating inverse functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). If f is a one-to-one differentiable function with inverse g = f^(-1), then g'(x) = 1/f'(g(x)), provided f'(g(x)) != 0. Equivalently, (f^(-1))'(b) = 1/f'(a) where f(a) = b. Key concepts: the derivative of an inverse function at a point is the reciprocal of the derivative of the original function at the corresponding point; graphically, since f and f^(-1) are reflections over y = x, their slopes at corresponding points are reciprocals. Students should apply this formula using tables of values: if given f(3) = 7 and f'(3) = 4, then (f^(-1))'(7) = 1/4. This concept connects to the derivatives of ln x (inverse of e^x) and inverse trigonometric functions (Topic 3.4). AP exam note: questions often provide tabular data for f and f' and ask for the derivative of f^(-1) at a specific point. Vocabulary: inverse function, one-to-one, reciprocal, corresponding points, reflection over y = x.

Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.skill: FUN-3.E Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions - 3.4 Differentiating Inverse Trigonometric Functions
Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.prompt: This topic covers derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Key formulas: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/sqrt(1-x^2), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/sqrt(1-x^2), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x^2). These can be derived using implicit differentiation: if y = arcsin x, then sin y = x, so cos y (dy/dx) = 1, giving dy/dx = 1/cos y = 1/sqrt(1-sin^2 y) = 1/sqrt(1-x^2). With the Chain Rule: d/dx[arctan(3x)] = 3/(1+9x^2). Students should know the domains and ranges of inverse trigonometric functions (arcsin and arccos have restricted domains [-1,1]; arctan has domain all reals). These derivatives are important for integration formulas involving 1/sqrt(1-x^2) and 1/(1+x^2). AP exam note: the derivative of arctan is most commonly tested; arcsin appears occasionally. Students should recognize these forms in both differentiation and antidifferentiation contexts. Vocabulary: inverse trigonometric, arcsine, arccosine, arctangent, restricted domain, implicit derivation.

Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.skill: FUN-3.C, FUN-3.D, FUN-3.E Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions - 3.5 Selecting Procedures for Calculating Derivatives
Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.prompt: This topic covers selecting appropriate differentiation procedures for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.C, FUN-3.D, FUN-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students must identify which rules to apply based on the structure of the function: Power Rule for polynomials, Product Rule for products, Quotient Rule for quotients, Chain Rule for compositions, implicit differentiation for implicit relations, and inverse function derivative formulas. Often multiple rules must be combined: e.g., d/dx[x^2 e^(sin x)] requires the Product Rule and Chain Rule together. Decision-making strategy: examine the outermost operation (sum, product, quotient, or composition) and apply the corresponding rule first. Students should practice with complex expressions that require multiple rules in sequence. This topic synthesizes all differentiation techniques from Units 2-3. AP exam note: the ability to quickly identify and apply the correct combination of rules is essential for time management on the exam. Vocabulary: procedure selection, Product Rule, Quotient Rule, Chain Rule, implicit differentiation, combined rules.

Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.skill: FUN-3.F Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions - 3.6 Calculating Higher-Order Derivatives
Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, an.prompt: This topic covers higher-order derivatives for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-3.F, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). The second derivative f''(x) is the derivative of f'(x), the third derivative f'''(x) is the derivative of f''(x), and so on. Notation: f''(x), d^2y/dx^2, y''. Key concepts: the second derivative gives the rate of change of the first derivative, which relates to concavity (f'' > 0 means concave up, f'' < 0 means concave down) and acceleration (if f represents position). Higher-order derivatives of polynomials eventually become zero; for sin x and cos x, derivatives cycle with period 4. Students should compute second and higher derivatives using all previously learned rules, including the Chain Rule and implicit differentiation (for d^2y/dx^2, differentiate dy/dx implicitly again). AP exam note: second derivatives are heavily tested in analyzing concavity, inflection points, and particle motion. The second derivative test for extrema also depends on this concept. Vocabulary: second derivative, higher-order derivative, concavity, acceleration, Leibniz notation, implicit second derivative.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: CHA-3.A Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.1 Interpreting the Meaning of the Derivative in Context
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers interpreting derivatives in real-world contexts for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). When f(t) represents a quantity in context (population, temperature, cost, etc.), f'(t) represents the instantaneous rate of change of that quantity with respect to t. Students must: (1) state what f'(t) represents in the context (e.g., 'the rate at which the population is changing at time t'), (2) include correct units (units of f divided by units of t), and (3) interpret the sign (positive means increasing, negative means decreasing). Key concepts: the derivative provides local information about how fast and in which direction a quantity is changing; the value f'(a) approximates the change in f for a small change in the input near a. AP exam note: free-response questions consistently require contextual interpretation with correct units. Stating 'the derivative is the rate of change' without specifying what is changing and with respect to what loses points. Vocabulary: rate of change, context, units, interpretation, increasing, decreasing, instantaneous.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: CHA-3.B Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.2 Straight-Line Motion: Connecting Position, Velocity, and Acceleration
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers straight-line (rectilinear) motion for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). If s(t) is the position of a particle at time t, then v(t) = s'(t) is velocity and a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) is acceleration. Key concepts: velocity is positive when the particle moves in the positive direction and negative when moving in the negative direction; speed is |v(t)|; the particle changes direction when velocity changes sign (v(t) = 0 and v changes sign); the particle speeds up when velocity and acceleration have the same sign and slows down when they have opposite signs. Students should analyze motion from position, velocity, or acceleration functions/graphs. Displacement over [a,b] is s(b) - s(a); total distance traveled requires considering direction changes. AP exam note: particle motion is one of the most common free-response topics. Students must clearly distinguish between displacement and total distance. Vocabulary: position, velocity, acceleration, speed, displacement, distance traveled, direction change.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: CHA-3.C Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.3 Rates of Change in Applied Contexts Other Than Motion
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers applying derivatives to rates of change in non-motion contexts for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students interpret derivatives in diverse contexts: population growth (dP/dt), temperature change (dT/dt), cost functions (marginal cost dC/dx), volume change (dV/dt), and more. Key concepts: identifying the independent and dependent variables, computing rates using differentiation rules, interpreting the sign and magnitude of the derivative in context, and including appropriate units. For example, if C(x) = 0.01x^2 + 5x + 100 represents cost in dollars for x items, then C'(x) = 0.02x + 5 gives the marginal cost (approximate cost of producing one additional item) in dollars per item. Students should connect the derivative to the behavior of the function (increasing/decreasing, fast/slow change). AP exam note: contextual interpretation with units is critical on free-response questions. Students should practice writing complete sentences interpreting derivatives. Vocabulary: applied rate of change, marginal cost, units, interpretation, context, independent variable, dependent variable.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: CHA-3.D Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.4 Introduction to Related Rates
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic introduces related rates problems for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Related rates problems involve two or more quantities that change over time and are connected by an equation. By differentiating the equation with respect to time t (using implicit differentiation and the Chain Rule), students relate the rates of change of the quantities. Key steps: (1) draw a diagram and identify variables, (2) write an equation relating the variables, (3) differentiate both sides with respect to t, (4) substitute known values and solve for the unknown rate. Example: for a circle with increasing radius, A = pi*r^2 gives dA/dt = 2*pi*r*(dr/dt). Students must distinguish between variables (which change with time) and constants. Common relationships come from geometry (Pythagorean theorem, volume formulas, similar triangles, trigonometry). AP exam note: related rates is a classic free-response topic. Students must clearly show the differentiation step and substitute only after differentiating. Vocabulary: related rates, implicit differentiation with respect to time, rate of change, variables, constants.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: CHA-3.E Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.5 Solving Related Rates Problems
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers solving related rates problems for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Building on Topic 4.4, students solve more complex related rates problems involving various geometric and physical scenarios. Common problem types: expanding/contracting circles and spheres, filling/draining tanks (cones, cylinders), ladder problems (Pythagorean theorem), shadow problems (similar triangles), and angle of elevation problems (trigonometry). Key strategies: choose the equation that directly relates the quantities whose rates you know and want to find; eliminate extra variables using geometric relationships before differentiating; pay attention to signs (negative rates for decreasing quantities). Students should express the final answer with correct units and interpret it in context. AP exam note: a common error is substituting known values before differentiating, which incorrectly treats variables as constants. Students must differentiate first, then substitute. Another error is using the wrong geometric formula. Vocabulary: related rates, geometric relationships, Pythagorean theorem, similar triangles, volume, surface area, rate.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: CHA-3.F Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.6 Approximating Values of a Function Using Local Linearity and Linearization
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers local linearity and linearization for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.F, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). The tangent line at x = a, L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a), provides a linear approximation of f(x) near x = a. This works because differentiable functions are approximately linear when zoomed in sufficiently (local linearity). Key concepts: the linearization L(x) is the best linear approximation near x = a; the approximation f(x) ~ L(x) is better for x closer to a; if f is concave up (f'' > 0), the linearization underestimates f; if concave down (f'' < 0), it overestimates. Example: approximate sqrt(4.1) using f(x) = sqrt(x) at a = 4: L(x) = 2 + (1/4)(x-4), so sqrt(4.1) ~ 2.025. This concept connects to differentials: dy = f'(x)dx approximates the change in f. AP exam note: linearization appears in both multiple-choice and free-response contexts. Students should state whether the approximation is an over- or underestimate using concavity. Vocabulary: linearization, tangent line approximation, local linearity, overestimate, underestimate, concavity, differential.

Contextual Applications of Differentiati.skill: LIM-4.A Contextual Applications of Differentiation - 4.7 Using L’Hospital’s Rule for Determining Limits of Indeterminate Forms
Contextual Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers L'Hopital's Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). L'Hopital's Rule states: if lim[x->c] f(x)/g(x) yields the indeterminate form 0/0 or infinity/infinity, and f and g are differentiable near c with g'(x) != 0 near c, then lim[x->c] f(x)/g(x) = lim[x->c] f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter limit exists or is infinite. Key concepts: the rule only applies to indeterminate forms 0/0 or infinity/infinity; other indeterminate forms (0*infinity, infinity-infinity, 0^0, 1^infinity, infinity^0) must be rewritten into 0/0 or infinity/infinity form first; the rule can be applied repeatedly if needed; always verify the indeterminate form before applying. Example: lim[x->0] sin(x)/x = lim[x->0] cos(x)/1 = 1. AP exam note: students must verify the indeterminate form to receive full credit. Using L'Hopital's Rule on non-indeterminate forms gives incorrect results. Vocabulary: L'Hopital's Rule, indeterminate form, 0/0, infinity/infinity, differentiable, verify conditions.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-1.B Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.1 Using the Mean Value Theorem
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers the Mean Value Theorem (MVT) for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-1.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). The MVT states: if f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists at least one c in (a,b) such that f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a). Geometrically, there is a point where the tangent line is parallel to the secant line connecting (a,f(a)) and (b,f(b)). Key concepts: both hypotheses (continuity on closed interval, differentiability on open interval) must be verified; the MVT guarantees existence but does not find c; Rolle's Theorem is a special case when f(a) = f(b), giving f'(c) = 0. Applications include proving that a function with zero derivative everywhere on an interval is constant, and justifying bounds on function values. AP exam note: free-response questions require students to verify hypotheses explicitly and state the conclusion in context. The MVT is often tested with tabular data. Vocabulary: Mean Value Theorem, Rolle's Theorem, continuous, differentiable, secant line, parallel tangent.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-1.C Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.2 Extreme Value Theorem, Global Versus Local Extrema, and Critical Points
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers the Extreme Value Theorem, extrema classification, and critical points for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-1.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). The Extreme Value Theorem (EVT) states: if f is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], then f attains both a global maximum and a global minimum on [a,b]. Critical points occur where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) is undefined (while f(c) is defined). Local (relative) extrema can only occur at critical points (interior) while global (absolute) extrema can also occur at endpoints. Key concepts: not every critical point is an extremum; the EVT requires continuity on a closed interval; global extrema are the largest and smallest values overall, while local extrema are extreme relative to nearby points. Students should find critical points algebraically by setting f'(x) = 0 and identifying where f' is undefined. AP exam note: students must distinguish between local and global extrema and understand that global extrema on closed intervals are guaranteed by the EVT. Vocabulary: Extreme Value Theorem, critical point, local extremum, global extremum, endpoint, continuous.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.3 Determining Intervals on Which a Function Is Increasing or Decreasing
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers determining where a function increases or decreases for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing. Key procedure: (1) find critical points by solving f'(x) = 0 and locating where f'(x) is undefined; (2) create a sign chart for f' using test points in each interval between critical points; (3) state intervals of increase (f' > 0) and decrease (f' < 0). Students should express answers as intervals using proper notation. The sign of f' determines the behavior of f: positive derivative means the function rises from left to right, negative means it falls. This analysis is foundational for finding relative extrema (Topics 5.4-5.5) and sketching graphs (Topic 5.8). AP exam note: sign charts are useful for work but the AP exam requires justification in words or inequalities, not just a sign chart. Vocabulary: increasing, decreasing, sign chart, critical point, test point, first derivative, interval.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.4 Using the First Derivative Test to Determine Relative (Local) Extrema
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers the First Derivative Test for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). The First Derivative Test states: if f' changes from positive to negative at x = c, then f has a relative maximum at c; if f' changes from negative to positive at c, then f has a relative minimum at c; if f' does not change sign at c, then c is not a relative extremum. Key procedure: find critical points, construct a sign chart for f', and analyze sign changes. This test is more informative than the Second Derivative Test because it works even when f''(c) = 0 or is undefined. Students should state results clearly: 'f has a relative maximum at x = c because f' changes from positive to negative.' AP exam note: justification must reference the sign change of f', not just that f'(c) = 0. Students should practice writing complete justifications. Vocabulary: First Derivative Test, relative maximum, relative minimum, sign change, critical point, justification.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.5 Using the Candidates Test to Determine Absolute (Global) Extrema
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers the Candidates Test (Closed Interval Method) for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). To find absolute (global) extrema of a continuous function on a closed interval [a,b]: (1) find all critical points of f in (a,b); (2) evaluate f at each critical point and at both endpoints a and b; (3) the largest value is the absolute maximum and the smallest is the absolute minimum. This method works because the EVT guarantees that continuous functions on closed intervals attain their extreme values, and extreme values can only occur at critical points or endpoints. Key concepts: the Candidates Test is the most reliable method for finding absolute extrema on closed intervals; it requires evaluating f (not f') at the candidates. Students should list all candidate x-values, compute f at each, and compare. AP exam note: this is a commonly tested procedure; students must evaluate f at all candidates including both endpoints. Vocabulary: Candidates Test, Closed Interval Method, absolute maximum, absolute minimum, endpoint, critical point, evaluate.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.6 Determining Concavity of Functions over Their Domains
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers concavity analysis for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). A function f is concave up on an interval where f''(x) > 0 (the graph bends upward, tangent lines lie below the curve) and concave down where f''(x) < 0 (the graph bends downward, tangent lines lie above the curve). An inflection point occurs where f changes concavity (f'' changes sign). Key procedure: (1) find where f''(x) = 0 or f''(x) is undefined; (2) test the sign of f'' in each interval; (3) identify inflection points where sign changes occur. Students should understand that f''(c) = 0 alone does not guarantee an inflection point (e.g., f(x) = x^4 at x = 0). Concavity connects to the rate of change of the slope: concave up means slopes are increasing, concave down means slopes are decreasing. AP exam note: concavity justifications must reference the sign of f'', and inflection points require a sign change in f''. Vocabulary: concave up, concave down, inflection point, second derivative, sign change, bending.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.7 Using the Second Derivative Test to Determine Extrema
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers the Second Derivative Test for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.7). The test states: if f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0, then f has a relative minimum at c; if f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0, then f has a relative maximum at c; if f''(c) = 0, the test is inconclusive (use the First Derivative Test instead). Key concepts: the test uses concavity at the critical point to determine the nature of the extremum; concave up (f'' > 0) at a critical point means the function curves upward, creating a minimum; concave down (f'' < 0) creates a maximum. The Second Derivative Test is often faster than the First Derivative Test but has the limitation of being inconclusive when f''(c) = 0. Students should know when to use each test. AP exam note: when using the Second Derivative Test, students must state both conditions: f'(c) = 0 AND f''(c) > 0 (or < 0). Vocabulary: Second Derivative Test, relative minimum, relative maximum, concavity, critical point, inconclusive.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.8 Sketching Graphs of Functions and Their Derivatives
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers sketching graphs of f, f', and f'' and understanding their relationships for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.8). Key relationships: where f is increasing, f' is positive (above x-axis); where f is decreasing, f' is negative; relative extrema of f correspond to zeros of f' (with sign changes); where f is concave up, f'' is positive and f' is increasing; where f is concave down, f'' is negative and f' is decreasing; inflection points of f correspond to extrema of f' and zeros of f'' (with sign changes). Students should practice: given the graph of f, sketch f'; given the graph of f', sketch possible f; given f'', determine concavity and inflection points of f. AP exam note: graph-matching questions between f, f', and f'' are common. Students should systematically check each correspondence (zeros, signs, increasing/decreasing patterns) between the graphs. Vocabulary: graph of derivative, sign correspondence, zeros, increasing/decreasing, concavity, inflection point.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.A Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.9 Connecting a Function, Its First Derivative, and Its Second Derivative
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers the comprehensive connections between f, f', and f'' for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.9). Students synthesize all analytical tools: f' provides information about increasing/decreasing behavior and extrema of f; f'' provides information about concavity and inflection points of f, as well as increasing/decreasing behavior of f'. Key connections: f' = 0 and f' changes sign means f has a relative extremum; f'' = 0 and f'' changes sign means f has an inflection point; f' is increasing means f is concave up; f' has a relative extremum means f has an inflection point. Students should analyze functions using all three levels simultaneously, creating a complete picture of the function's behavior. This synthesis is essential for curve sketching and optimization. AP exam note: free-response questions often require multi-step reasoning connecting f, f', and f''. Students should justify each conclusion with appropriate derivative information. Vocabulary: synthesis, first derivative analysis, second derivative analysis, behavior, justification, complete analysis.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.B Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.10 Introduction to Optimization Problems
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic introduces optimization problems for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.10). Optimization uses derivatives to find maximum or minimum values of functions in applied contexts. Key setup steps: (1) identify the quantity to be optimized (maximized or minimized); (2) write a function expressing that quantity in terms of one variable; (3) determine the domain (feasible interval) from physical constraints; (4) find critical points and evaluate the function at critical points and endpoints. Students learn to translate verbal descriptions into mathematical functions and identify constraints that reduce the problem to one variable. Common contexts include maximizing area or volume given fixed perimeter or material, minimizing distance or cost, and maximizing revenue or profit. AP exam note: optimization is a major free-response topic. Students must clearly define variables, state the function to optimize, show the domain, and justify that their answer is indeed a maximum or minimum. Vocabulary: optimization, maximize, minimize, constraint, objective function, feasible domain, applied context.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.C Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.11 Solving Optimization Problems
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers solving optimization problems for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.11). Building on the setup from Topic 5.10, students complete the solution: after expressing the quantity to optimize as a function of one variable on a feasible domain, find critical points by setting the derivative equal to zero, then use the Candidates Test (closed interval), First Derivative Test, or Second Derivative Test to confirm the extremum. Common problem types: maximizing the volume of a box with fixed surface area, minimizing the surface area of a can with fixed volume, maximizing the area of a region bounded by a fence and a river, minimizing travel time across different media (Snell's law analogy). Students must justify that their critical point gives the desired extremum, not just any extremum. AP exam note: full credit requires justification (not just finding f'=0). Include units in the final answer and verify the answer is physically reasonable. Vocabulary: optimization, critical point, justify, Candidates Test, First Derivative Test, Second Derivative Test, applied maximum/minimum.

Analytical Applications of Differentiati.skill: FUN-4.D, FUN-4.E Analytical Applications of Differentiation - 5.12 Exploring Behaviors of Implicit Relations
Analytical Applications of Differentiati.prompt: This topic covers analyzing implicit relations using derivatives for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-4.D, FUN-4.E, Unit 5, Topic 5.12). Students apply first and second derivative analysis to curves defined implicitly. Using dy/dx found by implicit differentiation, students can: find tangent and normal lines to implicit curves, determine where the curve has horizontal tangent lines (numerator of dy/dx = 0, denominator != 0) and vertical tangent lines (denominator of dy/dx = 0, numerator != 0), analyze increasing/decreasing behavior along the curve. The second derivative d^2y/dx^2 can be found by differentiating dy/dx implicitly (substituting the expression for dy/dx back in) to analyze concavity. Students must use the original equation to find the corresponding y-values when identifying specific points. AP exam note: implicit relation analysis appears in free-response questions; students must carefully handle the algebra of implicit second derivatives and clearly identify points on the curve. Vocabulary: implicit relation, horizontal tangent, vertical tangent, implicit second derivative, concavity of implicit curve.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: CHA-4.A Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.1 Exploring Accumulations of Change
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic introduces the concept of accumulation for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-4.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Accumulation of change is the idea that the total change in a quantity can be found by summing up small changes. If f(t) represents a rate of change, then the accumulation (total change) over [a,b] is the integral of f from a to b. Key concepts: connecting the area under a rate function to total change; if v(t) is velocity, the integral of v from a to b gives displacement; if R(t) is a production rate, the integral gives total production. Students should understand accumulation intuitively before formal Riemann sums: area under a positive rate curve represents total accumulation, and the net area (above minus below x-axis) gives net change. This provides motivation for the definite integral as a limit of sums. AP exam note: interpreting integrals as accumulated change in context is essential for free-response questions. Students must explain what the integral represents with correct units. Vocabulary: accumulation, total change, rate of change, area under curve, net change, integral.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: LIM-5.A Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.2 Approximating Areas with Riemann Sums
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers Riemann sums for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-5.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Riemann sums approximate the area under a curve by dividing [a,b] into n subintervals and summing the areas of rectangles. Types: Left Riemann Sum (use left endpoint of each subinterval for height), Right Riemann Sum (use right endpoint), Midpoint Riemann Sum (use midpoint), and Trapezoidal Sum (average of left and right endpoints, forming trapezoids). For equal subintervals of width Delta_x = (b-a)/n: Left sum = Delta_x * sum of f(x_i) for left endpoints. Key concepts: as n increases, the approximation generally improves; for an increasing function, left sums underestimate and right sums overestimate; for a decreasing function, the reverse. The trapezoidal sum averages left and right sums. Students should compute Riemann sums from functions, tables, and graphs. AP exam note: Riemann sum calculations from tables of values are very common on free-response questions, often with unequal subintervals. Vocabulary: Riemann sum, left sum, right sum, midpoint sum, trapezoidal sum, subinterval, partition, overestimate, underestimate.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: LIM-5.B, LIM-5.C Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.3 Riemann Sums, Summation Notation, and Definite Integral Notation
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers the connection between Riemann sums and the definite integral for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-5.B, LIM-5.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). The definite integral is defined as the limit of Riemann sums: integral from a to b of f(x)dx = lim[n->infinity] sum[i=1 to n] f(x_i*) * Delta_x, where the partition gets arbitrarily fine. Key concepts: summation notation (sigma notation) for expressing Riemann sums compactly; the definite integral exists (the limit converges) when f is continuous on [a,b]; the definite integral represents the net signed area between f and the x-axis (positive above, negative below). Students should understand the integral symbol, limits of integration (a = lower, b = upper), the integrand f(x), and the differential dx. The definite integral is a number, not a function. AP exam note: students should connect Riemann sums to definite integrals and recognize that the definite integral gives exact net area. Vocabulary: definite integral, limit of Riemann sums, summation notation, net signed area, integrand, limits of integration, differential.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-5.A Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.4 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Accumulation Functions
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1 (FTC1) for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-5.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). FTC1 states: if f is continuous on [a,b], then g(x) = integral from a to x of f(t)dt is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and g'(x) = f(x). In other words, the derivative of an accumulation function is the integrand evaluated at the upper limit. With the Chain Rule: d/dx[integral from a to h(x) of f(t)dt] = f(h(x)) * h'(x). Key concepts: the accumulation function g(x) gives the net area under f from a to x; g is increasing where f is positive and decreasing where f is negative; g has extrema where f changes sign; g has inflection points where f has extrema. This theorem connects differentiation and integration as inverse processes. AP exam note: analyzing accumulation functions from graphs of f is a classic free-response question. Students must relate properties of g to the graph of f. Vocabulary: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, accumulation function, integrand, upper limit, Chain Rule extension.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-5.A Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.5 Interpreting the Behavior of Accumulation Functions Involving Area
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers interpreting accumulation functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-5.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Given g(x) = integral from a to x of f(t)dt, students analyze g using the graph of f. Key relationships: g(a) = 0 (the integral from a to a is zero); g is increasing where f > 0 and decreasing where f < 0; g has a relative maximum where f changes from positive to negative and a relative minimum where f changes from negative to positive; g is concave up where f is increasing and concave down where f is decreasing; g has inflection points where f has relative extrema. To find g at specific points, students compute the integral as net area (using geometric shapes like triangles, rectangles, and semicircles from the graph of f). AP exam note: this is one of the most frequently tested topics on the AP exam. Students must clearly connect their reasoning about g to features of the graph of f. Vocabulary: accumulation function, net area, increasing, decreasing, concavity, relative extrema, inflection point, geometric area.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.A Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.6 Applying Properties of Definite Integrals
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers properties of definite integrals for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Key properties: (1) integral from a to a of f(x)dx = 0; (2) integral from a to b of f(x)dx = -integral from b to a of f(x)dx (reversing limits changes sign); (3) integral from a to b of [f(x)+g(x)]dx = integral from a to b of f(x)dx + integral from a to b of g(x)dx (linearity); (4) integral from a to b of c*f(x)dx = c * integral from a to b of f(x)dx; (5) integral from a to b of f(x)dx + integral from b to c of f(x)dx = integral from a to c of f(x)dx (additivity over intervals). Students should use these properties to evaluate integrals by breaking them apart or combining them, and to compute integrals from given information about related integrals. AP exam note: questions frequently provide values of several integrals and ask students to compute a related integral using properties. Vocabulary: properties of integrals, linearity, additivity, reversing limits, constant multiple, sum rule.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.B Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.7 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Definite Integrals
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2 (FTC2) for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). FTC2 states: if f is continuous on [a,b] and F is any antiderivative of f (F' = f), then integral from a to b of f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a). This provides a method to evaluate definite integrals by finding antiderivatives. Key concepts: F(b) - F(a) is written as F(x) evaluated from a to b, using the notation [F(x)] from a to b; any antiderivative works (the constant of integration cancels); this theorem transforms the problem of computing areas into finding antiderivatives. Example: integral from 0 to pi of sin(x)dx = [-cos(x)] from 0 to pi = -cos(pi) - (-cos(0)) = 1 + 1 = 2. AP exam note: students must find correct antiderivatives and evaluate accurately at both bounds. The Net Change Theorem follows: integral from a to b of f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a). Vocabulary: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2, antiderivative, evaluation, Net Change Theorem, bounds.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.C Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.8 Finding Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals: Basic Rules and Notation
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers basic antidifferentiation rules for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). The indefinite integral (antiderivative) of f(x) is written as integral of f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where F'(x) = f(x) and C is the constant of integration. Key rules: integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n != -1); integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C; integral of e^x dx = e^x + C; integral of sin(x)dx = -cos(x) + C; integral of cos(x)dx = sin(x) + C; integral of sec^2(x)dx = tan(x) + C; integral of csc^2(x)dx = -cot(x) + C; integral of sec(x)tan(x)dx = sec(x) + C; integral of csc(x)cot(x)dx = -csc(x) + C; integral of 1/(1+x^2)dx = arctan(x) + C; integral of 1/sqrt(1-x^2)dx = arcsin(x) + C. Students should recognize these as the reverse of derivative formulas and always include +C for indefinite integrals. AP exam note: forgetting +C is a common error. These basic antiderivatives are the foundation for all integration techniques. Vocabulary: antiderivative, indefinite integral, constant of integration, reverse differentiation, basic integration rules.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.D Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.9 Integrating Using Substitution
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers u-substitution for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.9). U-substitution reverses the Chain Rule: integral of f(g(x))*g'(x)dx = integral of f(u)du where u = g(x). Key steps: (1) identify the inner function u = g(x); (2) compute du = g'(x)dx; (3) rewrite the integral entirely in terms of u; (4) integrate with respect to u; (5) substitute back x. For definite integrals, either change the limits to u-values or substitute back after integrating. Students should recognize when substitution is appropriate: look for a composite function where the derivative of the inner function appears as a factor. Examples: integral of 2x*cos(x^2)dx with u = x^2; integral of e^(3x)dx with u = 3x (multiply/divide by constants as needed). AP exam note: u-substitution is one of the most frequently tested integration techniques. Students should be comfortable adjusting for missing constant factors. Vocabulary: u-substitution, change of variable, inner function, differential, reverse Chain Rule, change limits.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.D Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.10 Integrating Functions Using Long Division and Completing the Square
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers integration techniques involving long division and completing the square for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.10). Long division: when the degree of the numerator is greater than or equal to the degree of the denominator in a rational function, perform polynomial long division to rewrite the integrand as a polynomial plus a proper fraction, then integrate term by term. Example: integral of (x^2+1)/(x-1)dx: divide to get x + 1 + 2/(x-1), then integrate as x^2/2 + x + 2ln|x-1| + C. Completing the square: rewrite quadratic expressions in the denominator to match arctan or arcsin forms. Example: integral of 1/(x^2+4x+8)dx = integral of 1/((x+2)^2+4)dx = (1/2)arctan((x+2)/2) + C. Students should recognize when each technique is needed and execute the algebra accurately. AP exam note: these algebraic manipulations appear in multiple-choice and make otherwise intractable integrals computable. Vocabulary: long division, polynomial division, completing the square, proper fraction, arctan form, arcsin form.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.E Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.11 Integrating Using Integration by Parts
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers integration by parts for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.11). Integration by parts reverses the Product Rule: integral of u dv = uv - integral of v du. The LIATE guideline helps choose u (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential -- choose u from earlier in this list). Key applications: integral of x*e^x dx (u=x, dv=e^x dx), integral of x*sin(x)dx (u=x, dv=sin(x)dx), integral of ln(x)dx (u=ln(x), dv=dx), integral of x^2*e^x dx (apply twice). Sometimes integration by parts must be applied repeatedly, or the original integral reappears and can be solved algebraically. For definite integrals, apply the boundary evaluation to the uv term and the remaining integral. AP exam note: integration by parts appears on both AB and BC exams. Students should practice choosing u and dv efficiently and handling repeated applications. Vocabulary: integration by parts, LIATE, Product Rule reversal, tabular method, repeated application.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: FUN-6.F Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.12 Using Linear Partial Fractions
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers partial fraction decomposition for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.12). When integrating rational functions p(x)/q(x) where q(x) factors into distinct linear factors, decompose into partial fractions: A/(x-r1) + B/(x-r2) + ... Then each term integrates to a natural logarithm. Key steps: (1) factor the denominator completely; (2) write the decomposition with unknown constants; (3) solve for the constants using algebraic methods (multiplying both sides by the denominator and substituting convenient values of x or equating coefficients); (4) integrate each term separately. Example: integral of (3x+1)/((x-1)(x+2))dx decomposes as A/(x-1) + B/(x+2). This technique applies when the degree of the numerator is less than the degree of the denominator; otherwise, perform long division first. AP exam note: the AP exam limits partial fractions to distinct linear (non-repeated) factors. Students should practice the full process from factoring through integration. Vocabulary: partial fractions, decomposition, linear factors, coefficients, rational function, natural logarithm.

Integration and Accumulation of Change.skill: LIM-6.A Integration and Accumulation of Change - 6.13 Evaluating Improper Integrals
Integration and Accumulation of Change.prompt: This topic covers improper integrals for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.13). An integral is improper if: (1) one or both limits of integration are infinite, or (2) the integrand has an infinite discontinuity within [a,b]. Evaluation method: replace the infinite bound with a variable and take the limit. For type 1: integral from a to infinity of f(x)dx = lim[t->infinity] integral from a to t of f(x)dx. For type 2: if f has a discontinuity at c in [a,b], split into two improper integrals and evaluate each with a limit. If the limit exists and is finite, the integral converges; otherwise, it diverges. Key examples: integral from 1 to infinity of 1/x^p dx converges for p > 1 and diverges for p <= 1. Students should set up the limit, evaluate the integral, then take the limit. AP exam note: improper integrals appear in both AB and BC contexts.
